title-group.article-title: Clinical effectiveness and impact of 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccines in preventing invasive pneumococcal disease among children with risk conditions: A systematic literature review

contrib-group.aff: aGlobal Vaccines Medical Affairs, Pfizer, Inc., Collegeville, PA, USA
bUS Vaccines Medical Affairs, Pfizer, Inc., Collegeville, PA, USA
cHealth Economics and Outcomes Research, Pfizer Inc., New York, NY, USA
dFreelance Senior Systematic Reviewer, York, UK

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Christian Theilacker christian.theilacker@pfizer.com Pfizer Inc., 500 Arcola Road, Collegeville, PA 19426, USA.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 Pfizer Inc. Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Few studies have reported vaccine effectiveness (VE) and population-level impact of the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccination (PCV13) among children with immunocompromising or chronic medical conditions putting them at risk of developing pneumococcal disease. The objective of this systematic literature review (SLR) was to summarize the efficacy, effectiveness, and impact of PCV13 in the prevention of invasive pneumococcal disease (IPD) and all-cause pneumonia among children with risk conditions. Peer-reviewed studies published between January 2000 and July 2024 were identified in MEDLINE, Embase, CENTRAL, and CDSR. Of 3008 records identified, full texts of 189 studies (6.3%) were reviewed and 11 (0.4%) studies evaluating PCV13 alone or in combination with PCV7 were included. Vaccine effectiveness or impact of PCV13 was reported in children with sickle cell disease (SCD; n = 2), HIV (n = 4), post-transplant conditions (n = 1), cancer (n = 1), combined immunocompromising conditions (n = 1), combined chronic medical and immunocompromising conditions (n = 1), and congenital heart disease (n = 1). In children with immunocompromising conditions, VE against PCV13-type IPD ranged between 83.8% and 91%. Compared to the pre-PCV era, the introduction of PCV13 in pediatric immunization programs was associated with a relative reduction of IPD due to any serotype between 86% and 100% in children SCD and 55% to 80% in children with cancer. PCV impact, as measured by incidence rate reductions, among children with HIV varied by comparison period, age group and outcome and ranged from 9.1% to 81% for any IPD. These findings support PCV vaccination for children with risk conditions although evidence was limited and heterogeneous.

funding-group.funding-statement: This systematic literature review was sponsored by Pfizer Inc.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus) in children is responsible for pneumonia and invasive pneumococcal diseases (IPD), such as bacteremia, sepsis, and meningitis. In addition, pneumococcus is a common cause of mucosal infections, such as otitis media, mastoiditis, and conjunctivitis.1–3 In 2022, there were 13.4 and 10.8 cases of IPD per 100,000 children <1 year of age across Europe and the United States (US), respectively.4,5 The burden of IPD is not uniform across all pediatric populations.6 Children with certain underlying medical conditions are at substantially higher risk of pneumococcal disease, with reported rates as high as 72 times higher with sickle cell disease and 41 times higher with any immunosuppression compared to children of the same age without these medical conditions.7–9

In 2023, the US Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) updated guidance to define children with risk conditions as those with underlying chronic medical conditions and immunocompromising conditions.10 An analysis of US databases found that the percentage of US children <18 years with IPD who had an underlying risk factor increased from 1998–1999 to 2014–2018 in both those with commercial insurance (14.3% to 44.6%) and in those insured by the government (31.1% to 56.0%).11 In this analysis, the most common risk conditions for IPD were chronic lung disease including asthma (22.3%), cancer and iatrogenic immunosuppression, including radiation therapy (21.3%), congenital or acquired immunodeficiency (19.6%), and chronic heart disease (12.1%).

The seven-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV7) was the first PCV universally recommended in the US in 2000, and several years later in European countries. It was replaced by the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) in 2009/10 across Europe and the US. PCV13 vaccination schedules differed by country with two or three infant doses with or without a booster (3 + 1, 2 + 1, or 3 + 0) when used in a national immunization program.12–14 Over twenty years of PCV use has shown the vaccines to be safe and effective in children within the general population, with a significant reduction in the risk of disease and mortality.15–17 A literature review of 58 trials (9 immunocompetent, 39 immunocompromised, and 12 asplenic) assessed the safety and immunogenicity of PCV7. It found PCV7 safe and immunogenic in children and adults at higher risk than the general population.18 Although PCV13 induces robust immunogenicity in those with risk conditions, responses are more variable and lower than those of healthy populations.19

Since the risk conditions in the general population are relatively infrequent, few studies report vaccine effectiveness or impact of PCV13 in those with risk conditions. Small sample size frequently results in underpowered analyses. Systematic literature reviews (SLRs) address this limitation by summarizing and evaluating evidence across all available studies.20 To date, SLRs on PCV13 efficacy and effectiveness against IPD in children have focused on healthy children21–24 or a specific risk group, such as HIV-positive children.25 Chilson et al. conducted an SLR on patients across multiple immunocompromising conditions, but it only reported PCV13 immunogenicity and safety and included adult and pediatric populations.19
The primary objective of this SLR was to evaluate the efficacy, effectiveness, and impact of PCV13 on IPD in children with any condition at increased risk of pneumococcal disease.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: This SLR was conducted in accordance with the methods recommended by the Cochrane Collaboration,26 where relevant, and was part of a broader SLR that also evaluated PCV7, immunogenicity, and nasopharyngeal carriage outcomes. Results reporting PCV13 efficacy, effectiveness, and impact are presented here, although no eligible efficacy data, i.e. clinical protection measured in the context of a placebo-controlled randomized trial as the ‘‘per protocol” efficacy, were identified. A meta-analysis was not conducted due to the high levels of heterogeneity across study designs, time periods, vaccine schedules, age groups, settings, and outcome definitions. The review is reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA).27

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: The search included articles published from January 2000 to July 2024 in OvidSP to identify published peer-reviewed studies of interest in the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Controlled Trials Register Search Strategy (CENTRAL), and Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews (CDSR) electronic literature databases. Searches were performed using a combination of MeSH/EMTREE indexing terms and free-text terms specific to each database as recommended by the Cochrane Collaboration. The individual search strategies for MEDLINE, Embase, CENTRAL, and CDSR are shown in Tables S1–S4.

The bibliographies of three other reviews were cross-checked to ensure key eligible studies were not missed.7,25,28 A search was conducted for contemporary conference abstracts (2019 to 2024) indexed in Embase via OvidSP to identify relevant research that may not have been fully published. Search results were de-duplicated using EndNote X9 software (Clarivate Analytics, London, UK) and subsequently managed, triaged, and assessed using Nested Knowledge software (Nested Knowledge Inc).

sec.title: Definition of risk groups
sec.p: Risk groups were defined according to ACIP guidelines (see Supplementary Table S6) according to criteria published elsewhere.10 When compared to previous ACIP definitions of risk groups, the revised definitions classify chronic kidney disease with maintenance dialysis or nephrotic syndrome as immunocompromising conditions. Also, chronic liver disease was added as a risk condition. Furthermore, moderate or severe persistent asthma is classified as a risk condition regardless of corticosteroid use.10,29

sec.title: PICOS inclusion criteria
sec.p: Inclusion criteria were defined using a PICOS (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcomes, Study design) framework.30 Briefly, observational studies or randomized controlled trials (RCTs) were included if they reported on effectiveness, efficacy, and/or impact of PCV13 vaccination in children with underlying medical conditions and increased risk of pneumococcal disease. Further details are presented in Table S5.

sec.title: Screening
sec.p: Screening was a two-stage process conducted by two independent reviewers. Disagreements were resolved by a third independent reviewer, as necessary. First, titles and abstracts were reviewed against the PICOS selection criteria and any article meeting one or more exclusion criteria was excluded. Full-text articles were retrieved for all the remaining titles/abstracts. Second, full-text articles were examined in further detail and included in the review if they met all the PICOS inclusion criteria and none of the exclusion criteria. Reason for exclusion was recorded for excluded full-text articles. The study selection process was recorded in accordance with the PRISMA checklist.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data were extracted independently by one reviewer into a Microsoft Excel template and a second reviewer validated all entries. Disputes were resolved by a third reviewer. Information was extracted on the study design, population description, vaccination characteristics, and outcome data. Extracted efficacy and effectiveness outcomes included estimated vaccine efficacy/effectiveness and the proportion of children experiencing any IPD events. Extracted impact outcomes included the relative risk (RR) ratio, incidence rate ratio (IRR), rate difference, percentage change pre-/post-vaccination of S. pneumoniae-related IPD (all-cause and vaccine serotype-specific) for all events and specific events (meningitis, pneumonia, septicemia). Only outcomes recorded at the end of each study were extracted. Results were extracted separately for populations with underlying risk conditions (chronic medical conditions and/or immunocompromising conditions). Data were also stratified by geographical location, age category (infant <2 years; young child 2–5 years; older child 6–18 years), and vaccine dosing schedule (3 + 1 doses, 2 + 1 doses, 3 + 0 doses). The preferred population was the intention-to-treat population, and the preferred analysis was the multivariate adjusted analysis. Data were extracted as reported, where possible, or calculated otherwise e.g. impact estimates were calculated based on pre- and post-vaccination incidence estimates and used to generate the relative or absolute risk reduction.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The quality of included full-text studies was assessed with the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS), which is a tool used to assess the quality of non-randomized studies, particularly cohort and case–control studies.31 It evaluates studies based on three main criteria: the selection of study groups, the comparability of groups, and outcome assessment. The questions from the original NOS were adapted to align with the scope of this SLR (Supplemental Methods).

sec.title: Study selection and characteristics
sec.p:
Of the 3008 records identified, 189 full-text studies (6.3%) were reviewed and 11 studies (0.4%) were included in the final analysis (Figure 1).15,32–41 All studies were observational; nine were cohort studies33–41 and two were case–control studies (Table 1).15,32 All studies evaluated the use of PCV13 alone or in combination with PCV7 in children with risk conditions. Nine studies reported either impact or VE against IPD and two studies reported impact or VE against an all-cause outcome. Two studies evaluated effectiveness and eight evaluated impact. Four studies evaluated PCV13 in children with HIV,15,36–38 two studies in children sickle cell disease (SCD),33,34 and one study each in children with cancer,35 immunocompromising conditions,40 post-transplant conditions,32 combined diseases and immunocompromised conditions,39 and congenital heart disease.41 Six studies analyzed data from national surveillance or health insurance databases,15,32,33,36,38,39 and five used data from regional or hospital databases.32,34,35,37,40 Four studies were conducted in South Africa,15,36–38 four in the US,32–35 and one each in Canada,40 Mexico,41 and Taiwan.39 Figure 1.
PRISMA flow chart. *PDF files unavalabile when literatyre review was conducted. †Medical letter (n=1) and clinicalTrials.govrecord (n=1).Table 1.

aChronic heart, lung, liver disease, diabetes, asthma, HIV, renal failure, nephrotic syndrome, congenital immunodeficiency disease of white blood cells, cochlear implant, malignant neoplasms, solid organ transplantation. bTransplant (solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell), malignancy, primary immunodeficiency, asplenia, sickle cell disease (SCD), cochlear implant, nephrotic syndrome, chronic lung disease, CSF leak, HIV and use of immunosuppressive therapy (chemotherapy, biologic drugs or immune-suppressing medications) in the 90 days preceding IPD.
*Includes percentage change in IPD events.
†A level change refers to an abrupt shift in the average value of a time series immediately following an intervention, while a trend change involves a modification in the rate of change (slope) of a time series over time after an intervention.
Abbreviations: CDC, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; HIV, human immunodeficiency virus; IPD, invasive pneumococcal disease; IRR, incidence rate ratio; NA, not applicable; NR, not reported; OR, odds ratio; PCR, polymerase chain reaction; PCV, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; pts, patients; RR, relative risk; VE, vaccine effectiveness; VT, vaccine type.

The main quality concerns for the two case–control studies were the relatively small sample sizes and the lack of information on how the required sample sizes were determined (Table S6).15,32 Quality assessment scores for the eight cohort studies (Table S7) and one cross-sectional study (Table S8) were generally similar. Limitations of these studies may have included small sample size,34 changes in reporting systems, diagnostic criteria, or healthcare access over time,33,35 and lack of generalizability for studies conducted at a single hospital33–35,40,41 or regionally.32,36

sec.title: PCV13 protection in children with immunocompromising conditions
Effectiveness
sec.p:
Two vaccine effectiveness studies reported effectiveness against PCV13-type IPD, PCV13non7-type IPD and any IPD. Due to small sample size, 95% confidence intervals were broad and included zero in both studies (Table 2).15,32 First, Cohen et al. evaluated 358 HIV-infected children aged ≥16 weeks in South Africa who received ≥2 doses of PCV13 alone or in combination with PCV7 and compared them to children who did not receive PCV in a matched case–control study.15 Children were identified in 24 hospitals participating in sentinel surveillance, with cases enrolled from a national surveillance database. The effectiveness of two or more doses of PCV13 against PCV13-serotype IPD was 91% (95% CI −35, 100) among children with HIV infection, which was comparable to PCV13 VE in HIV uninfected children from the same study (VE 85%, 95% confidence interval 37, 96).Table 2.
PCV13 or PCV 7 effectiveness in IPD.
Author (year)Underlying risk conditionPopulation (number of cases)Doses receivedVaccine and comparatorOutcomeVaccine Effectiveness
Immunocompromising conditions

PCV13 vs. no vaccinePCV13non7-type IPDc82.0 (−155.0 to 100.0)a
PCV13 vs. no vaccineAny IPD3% (−1630 to 93)a

4 dosesPCV13 or PCV7 vs no vaccineVT-type IPD83.8 (−44.1 to 98.2)a
Risk groups with chronic medical conditions and immunocompromising conditions
van Warmderdam (2023)40Any listed high-risk conditiondChildren aged <18 years with known vaccination status (N = 66)15 patients unvaccinated31 patients received 1–3 doses20 patients received 4 dosesPCV13 fully (≥3 doses) vs. partially vaccinated or unvaccinatedPCV13-type IPD97.3e
aAdjusted vaccine effectiveness (95% CI). bAmong 75 cases with HIV infection aged ≥16 weeks in the PCV13 period, 75% PCV7-type IPD and 54% of PCV13-type cases received 2 or more doses. Not everyone received 2 doses. cSerotypes 1, 3, 5, 6A, 7F, and 19A. dTransplant (solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell), malignancy, primary immunodeficiency, asplenia, sickle cell disease (SCD), cochlear implant, nephrotic syndrome, chronic lung disease, CSF leak, HIV and use of immunosuppressive therapy (chemotherapy, biologic drugs or immune-suppressing medications) in the 90 days preceding IPD. eUnadjusted value comparing fully vaccinated individuals versus individuals who were not vaccinated or only partially vaccinated.
Abbreviations: CI, confidence interval; IPD, invasive pneumococcal disease; NR, not reported; PCV, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; VE, vaccine effectiveness; VT, vaccine type.

Second, Olarte et al. evaluated PCV13 effectiveness using the indirect cohort method among 65 hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) or solid organ transplant recipients with IPD.32 Most IPD cases were children (68%) and had solid organ transplants, primarily heart and liver transplants. Vaccine effectiveness against VT IPD was 52% (95% CI −68.1, 86.3) for children who received at least one dose of PCV13 or PCV7 and 83.8% (−44.1, 98.2) for children who received four doses of PCV13 or PCV7.

sec.title: Impact
sec.p:
Six studies that reported on the impact of PCV13 on IPD showed that PCV13 vaccination reduced IPD incidence across various populations, including children with SCD, HIV-positive children, and children with cancer, with rate reductions ranging from 9% to nearly 100% depending on the group and comparison period (Table 3 and Supplementary Table S9).33–38Table 3.
PCV13 impact on IPD.
Author (year)Risk conditionAgePCV13 periodComparator periodOutcomeRelative reduction(95% CI)P-valueAbsolute rate reduction per 100K(95% CI)
Adamkiewicz (2023)33SCD/HbSS0–9 y.2010–2018pre-PCV(1994–1999)Any IPD86.2 (75 to 92.4)p < .00118.1b
SCD/HbSC86.2 (55.8 to 95.7)p < .00111.3 b

SCD/HbSS/SCPCV13non7 IPDc100 (91.9 to 100)p < .0011.5b

aSCD. bValues were calculated. cData for PCV7 serotypes (4, 6B, 9 V, 14, 18C, 19F, and 23F) and serotype 6A were also reported in the study, but these are not included here. dTransplant (solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell), malignancy, primary immunodeficiency, asplenia, sickle cell disease (SCD), cochlear implant, nephrotic syndrome, chronic lung disease, CSF leak, HIV and use of immunosuppressive therapy (chemotherapy, biologic drugs or immune-suppressing medications) in the 90 days preceding IPD.
Abbreviations: CI, confidence interval; Hb, hemoglobin; Hb SC, sickle cell disease (less severe form); Hb SS, sickle cell disease (more severe form); HIV, human immunodeficiency virus; IPD, invasive pneumococcal disease; NR, not reported; PCV, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; SCD, sickle cell disease; VT, vaccine type.

A study by Adamkiewicz et al. of 3707 US children <9 years with SCD under surveillance in the Georgia Emerging Infections Program reported relative rate reductions for IPD due to any serotype around 86%, and resulting absolute rate reductions between 11 and 18 IPD cases per 100,000 children with SCD (Table 3). Relative rate reductions for IPD due to PCV13non7 serotype were 100% (91.9, 100).33 IPD impact was largely consistent across age groups and SCD subgroups (HbSS, HbSC) and comparable to children without SCD. Relative rate reductions were moderately smaller, when incidence rates for any IPD in the PCV13 period were compared to the PCV7 period (Supplementary Table S9). For this comparison period, relative rate reductions in children <9 years of age HbSS, HbSC, and HbSC/HbSS mutations were 60.9 (95% CI 27.8 to 78.8), −9.1 (95% CI −79.7 to 75.4) and 54.9 (95% CI 21.1 to 74.2).

A single center study at the Children’s Hospitals and Clinics of Minnesota conducted by Martin et al. reported incidence rates during the PCV13 and PCV7 eras (Table 3).34 While incidence was numerically higher in the PCV13 era (493 per 100,000 person years) than in the PCV7 era (349 per 100,000 person years), estimates were based on few cases (7 cases during the PCV7 era and 4 cases during the PCV13 era) and only one case was due to a serotype included in a PCV recommended at the time.

Three studies from South Africa reported the combined impact of the introduction of PCV7 in 2009 and PCV13 in 2011 on IPD incidence captured by nationwide surveillance in children with HIV (Table 3).36–38 The first analysis by Nzenze et al. compared IPD incidence during the PCV13 period (2012) to the PCV7 period (2010) by age group. In children aged 3 months to 2 years, PCV13 introduction was associated with a 9.1% (−129.5, 64.8) reduction of IPD due to any serotype, a 45.5% (−155, 91.2) reduction of PCV7-type IPD and a 100% reduction of PCV13non7-type IPD.36 In children aged 2–5 years, the corresponding relative reductions of IPD due to any serotype, PCV7 serotypes and PCV13non7 serotypes were 60.1% (−24.4, 89.1), 12.3% (−120.0, 98.9) and 43.0% (−167.0, 90.8). A second analysis by von Gottberg et al. compared incidence rates during the PCV13 period (2012) to the pre-PCV era (2005–2008) demonstrated among children <2 years of age an incidence rate reduction of 77% (70, 83), 72% (44, 88), 86% (78, 91) for IPD due to any serotypes, PCV7 serotypes, and PCV13non7 serotypes respectively.37 A third analysis by von Mollendorf et al. compared IPD incidence in 2013, i.e. two years after the introduction of PCV13 with incidence rates in 2009, the year of PCV7 introduction by age group and HIV status.38 Incidence rate reduction for IPD due to any serotype among HIV infected children in the first year of life was 58.4% (51.5, 64.9), with similar rate reductions in the age groups <6 months (49.7% [38.8, 60.0]) and 6–12 months (68.1% [58.8, 76.6]). Studies also reported impressive absolute rate reductions ranging between 318 and 575 IPD cases per 100,000 children with HIV, which was approximately 10-fold higher than in HIV exposed, but uninfected children (Table 3).

A US single center cohort study by Lee et al. compared the incidence of IPD due to any serotype in post-PCV13 period (2011–2012) to the pre-PCV period (1992–2000) among 343 children fully vaccinated children overall and by cancer type.35 Among children with any cancer, relative rate reductions in children aged 1–4 years and aged 5–14 years were 80% and 55%, respectively. Measures of statistical significance or precision were not reported. PCV13 impact was similar in children with hematologic malignancies and with solid cancers (Table 3) and overall reductions were less pronounced when the PCV13 period was compared to the PCV7 period (2001 to 2010).

sec.title: Studies including children with chronic medical conditions or immunocompromising conditions
Effectiveness
sec.p: A matched case–control study by van Warmerdam et al. assessed PCV13 effectiveness among 94 children with IPD aged 0–18 years with risk conditions identified by the Toronto Invasive Bacterial Disease Network at a large pediatric hospital in Toronto between 2009 and 2018 (Table 2).40 In this population with 93% immunocompromising diseases or medication, VE of PCV13 against vaccine-type IPD was 97.3%. However, this study did not report the method used for calculation of vaccine effectiveness and effectiveness estimates were unadjusted.

sec.title: Impact
sec.p:
In children <18 years with high-risk conditions in the Toronto Invasive Bacterial Disease Network, IPD rates due to PCV13 serotypes were 89% lower in the early PCV13 period (2011 to 2015) and 100% lower in the late PCV13 period (2016 to 2018) when compared to the pre-PCV13 period (2009 to 2010) (Table 3).40 A study by Huang et al. aimed to assess the impact of the national PCV13 catch-up program following the introduction of PCV7 on pneumonia incidence among the overall and high-risk populations in Taiwan using an interrupted time series analysis. The impact of a catch-up program consisting of a single dose of PCV13 among Taiwanese children <18 years with chronic medical conditions or immunocompromising conditions was assessed using the National Health Insurance Research Database (Table 1).39 Actual and expected incidence trends of all-cause pneumonia and pneumococcal pneumonia during the catch-up period 2013 to 2015 were compared to the pre-catch up period (2001–2012). Trends were measured by level change, abrupt shifts in the average value of a time series immediately following an intervention, and trend change, a modification in the rate of change (slope) of a time series over time after an intervention (Table 4). The PCV13 catch-up program was associated with 40.4, 301.7 240.0 and 70.1 fewer cases of all-cause CAP per 100,000 per 3 months of observation in children aged 0–1 years, 2–4 years, 5–9 years, and 10–17 years although p-values for all rate changes estimates were >0.05. Absolute rate reductions for pneumococcal pneumonia were −2.5, 10.6, 1.9 and 1.4 per 100,000 person-quarter.Table 4.
PCV13 impact on all-cause pneumonia.
Author (year) Population (Sample size)Vaccine and comparatorChange in incidence rate of all-cause pneumonia per 100,000 person-quarter*
Huang (2022)39All-risk populationaAge group 0–1 y (NR)Post- (2013–2015) vs Pre- (2001–2012) a PCV13 catch-up programLevel change: 40.40; p = .86Trend change: −4.86; p = .87
Age group 2–4 y. (NR)Level change: −301.65; p = .26Trend change: 5.25; p = .87
Age group 5–9 y. (NR)Level change: −239.98; p = .19Trend change: 2.61; p = .91
Age group 10–17 y. (NR)Level change: −70.14; p = .24Trend change: 2.81; p = .70
Chronic conditions populationAge group 0–1 y (NR)Level change: 56.55; p = .81Trend change: −8.59; p = .77
Age group 2–4 y. (NR)Level change: −288.32; p = .28Trend change: 5.63; p = .86
Age group 5–9 y. (NR)Level change: −237.05; p = .19Trend change: 3.09; p = .89
Age group 10–17 y. (NR)Level change: −66.94; p = .26Trend change: 3.37; p = .65
Immunocompromising condition populationAge group 0–1 y (NR)Level change: 73.26; p = .90Trend change: 28.13; p = .69
Age group 2–4 y. (NR)Level change: −433.45; p = .29Trend change: −28.56; p = .58
Age group 5–9 y. (NR)Level change: −327.93; p = .22Trend change: −27.94; p = .40
Age group 10–17 y. (NR)Level change: −76.43; p = .33Trend change: −19.02; p = .06
aChronic heart, lung, liver disease, diabetes, asthma, HIV, renal failure, nephrotic syndrome, congenital immunodeficiency disease of white blood cells, cochlear implant, malignant neoplasms, solid organ transplantation.
*A level change refers to an abrupt shift in the average value of a time series immediately following an intervention, while a trend change involves a modification in the rate of change (slope) of a time series over time after an intervention.
Abbreviations: ACP, all-cause pneumonia; CI, confidence interval; HIV, human immunodeficiency virus; NR, not reported; PCV, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; RR, relative risk.

sec.title: Effectiveness
sec.p: PCV13 effectiveness against all-cause pneumonia or bronchopneumonia in children with congenital heart disease was reported by a single center cohort study from Mexico by Solórzano-Santos et al., which was conducted between January 2008 and December 2013 (Table 1).41 The study included 196 children <5 years of age who had received at least two doses of PCV13 or PCV7 and compared them to 152 unexposed children (22 with no vaccine doses and 130 with one dose). Fewer all-cause bronchopneumonia events occurred in vaccinated children than in unvaccinated children 13.3% [26/196] vs. 33.6% [51/152]; p < .001). The unadjusted relative risk reduction in VE for PCV13 for all-cause bronchopneumonia was 60.5% (39.7, 74). No adjusted VE analyses or PCV13-specific analyses were reported.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic literature review provides evidence supporting PCV13 direct protection against IPD in children with HIV and SCD with additional strong and consistent evidence for population-level impact in children with these immunocompromising conditions and other risk conditions. In children with immunocompromising conditions, vaccine effectiveness against PCV13-type IPD was high (83.8% to 91%), regardless of risk condition, though not statistically significant due to small sample sizes. PCV13 vaccination reduced IPD incidence in children with SCD, HIV, or cancer by 9% to 100%. PCV13 national immunization programs were associated with large absolute reductions of IPD among children with HIV, and absolute reductions were approximately 10-fold higher than in HIV negative children.

A total of 11 observational studies were included, mostly cohort studies with two using a case–control design. Vaccine effectiveness estimates against IPD in immunocompromised children were available for children with HIV living in South Africa and assessment of PCV13 impact were limited to studies from South Africa, North America, and Taiwan. Overall evidence from our systematic literature review supports that direct protection and overall impact of PCV13 against IPD and all-cause pneumonia in children with chronic medical conditions and immunocompromising conditions was high and generally comparable with PCV13 effect reported for the general population.17–43 These findings align with a previous SLR and meta-analysis that evaluated the efficacy and effectiveness of PCVs against IPD among HIV-infected and HIV-uninfected children younger than 7 years old.25

Serotype distribution varies over time and between countries due to differences in local vaccination recommendations, the introduction of PCV vaccinations, the availability of vaccines, and vaccine adoption by individual countries. Studies from the US,11,44,45 UK,46 Sweden,47 and South Africa15,36–38 show that an increasing proportion of IPD now occurs in children with immunocompromising conditions and chronic medical conditions and that IPD in these risk groups is more frequently caused by non-PCV13 serotypes than in healthy, immunocompetent children. A US study from 1998 to 2018 reported a rise in children with IPD who have underlying risk conditions, contributing to about 50% of IPD cases in children in the late PCV13 period.11 Data from the UK suggest that children with comorbidities were more likely to develop IPD due non-PCV13 serotypes than healthy children.48 About 25% of IPD cases occurred in children with immunocompromising conditions, cochlear implants, or cerebrospinal fluid leaks. These findings emphasize the need for protecting at-risk children to enhance PCV vaccination’s impact on public health.

Data presented here are consistent with the effect of PCV7 and PCV9 in children with risk conditions. A study by Adamkiewicz et al. of 1247 children ≤10 years with SCD showed that the incidence of IPD fell by 68% between 1995–2000 and 2001–2002, after the introduction of PCV7.49 In South Africa, the PCV9 vaccine reduced the incidence of IPD by 65% in HIV-infected children and by 83% in non-HIV-infected children. Additionally, for non-HIV-infected children, the vaccine decreased alveolar consolidation by 20–25%, reduced penicillin-resistant pneumococcal disease by 67%, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole-resistant strains by 56%.50

PCV20 is recommended for children with immunocompromising conditions and defined chronic medical conditions in the US, Europe, and Canada.10,51–52 While immunogenicity of PCV20 has not been studied in children with an immunocompromising condition or chronic medical condition, results from this SLR on PCV13 protection of risk-populations and PCV20 clinical trial data provide evidence that PCV20 will likely extend the protection documented for PCV13 to the seven additional serotypes included in its formulation.53

This SLR has several strengths. Comprehensive literature searches were conducted, encompassing a broad search period since the introduction of the first PCV in 2000. Furthermore, various key outcomes are included, and populations are not restricted by underlying risk conditions. Additionally, the restriction to published peer-reviewed studies likely ensured that the evidence was of the highest quality and methodologically sound. There are, however, limitations. Firstly, the review is confined to studies published in the English language. Although non-English language publications were excluded, these publications were noted during the screening process, with no significant publications evidently omitted. The findings of the SLR are also constrained by the heterogeneity across the included studies due to variations in study designs, time periods, population ages, vaccine schedules, outcome definitions, and study settings. This diversity precluded meta-analysis and limited the results to a narrative summary, making it challenging to compare findings across studies, as noted by previous SLRs in this field.24 Also, the quality of the studies varied as most of the studies were observational, with few concerns except for the potential lack of statistical power and generalizability of those studies from single hospitals or smaller regional samples versus national surveillance databases. VE estimates against IPD in immunocompromised children were only available for children with HIV living in South Africa, and assessment of PCV13 impact was limited to studies from South Africa, North America, and Taiwan. Furthermore, some VE results were unadjusted and should be interpreted with caution. Lastly, evidence for non-immunocompromising risk conditions was limited and of lower quality.

These findings support PCV vaccination for immunocompromised children. Given the high burden of pneumococcal disease in children with risk conditions, and the higher prevalence of serotypes only included in PCV20, PCV20 vaccination of this population should be a priority as well as continuing to monitor the impact of PCV20 immunization programs.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors would like to thank Adriano Arguedas of Pfizer for his project management and review of this manuscript. Medical writing was provided by John Plant of Evidera and was funded by Pfizer.
Conceptualization: KH, MT, LJ; data curation: RC, Formal analysis: RC, quality checks MT, RC, CT, visualization: MT, manuscript drafting and review: CT, RC, LH, CF, IP, MT, KH.

bio.p: Christian Theilacker, MD, PhD, MSc is the Global Medical Strategy and Team Lead for Pneumococcal Vaccine at Pfizer. He has held multiple leadership roles in global scientific and medical affairs within Pfizer since 2016. Prior to joining industry, Dr Theilacker served as a senior staff physician in infectious diseases at University Hospital Basel and University Medical Center Freiburg. He holds a licensure in infectious diseases and internal medicine and a Master’s degree in Epidemiology.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: KH, MJT, LH, and CT are employees of Pfizer. CF, IP, and RC are current or former employees of Evidera, which received funding from Pfizer in connection with the development of this manuscript and to conduct the systematic literature review.

sec.title: Consent for publication
sec.p: All authors have approved the final version of the article.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: This systematic literature exclusively utilizes publicly available data from previously published studies, reports, and articles. No new data was collected from human participants, and therefore, ethical approval from an institutional review board (IRB) or ethics committee was not required.

sec.title: Supplementary material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2614133
